cards.text: Malaysia kept SMEs out of mandatory e-invoicing by exempting under RM1m. Three peers kept theirs in with different instruments.
cards.sub: Singapore offers grants. India sets a higher threshold. Thailand allows email-based invoicing for small firms.

cards.h: Threshold comparison: India's mandate starts at RM2.8m; Thailand's email-route covers up to RM3.8m
cards.s: India's e-invoicing kicks in at INR 5 crore (~RM2.8m); Thailand's email-route is available for firms under THB 30m (~RM3.8m). Both let SMEs operate in the digital trail without full XML integration.
cards.d: India source: CBIC Notification 10/2023, effective 1 Aug 2023. Thailand source: Revenue Department guidelines; Sovos summary. Indonesia's VAT/e-Faktur threshold sits at IDR 4.8 billion (~RM1.36m) — below it firms are not required to register or issue e-invoices, but the UMKM regime offers a 0.5% final-tax rate.

cards.h: Singapore subsidises adoption: up to S$1,000 grant per SME plus free InvoiceNow-ready software until 31 March 2027
cards.s: Up to S$5,000 grant for early adopters. Rollout is progressive 2025-2031. Malaysia's MSME Digital Grant offers a 50% match up to RM5,000 plus tax deductions on e-invoice costs.
cards.d: Source: IRAS GST InvoiceNow Requirement page (primary). Corrected against Stage 4 of parent issue 1984 which had stated FOC packages run until 2031 — that date is the rollout end-point, not the FOC window.

cards.h: Thailand offers a graduated technical path: PDF-by-email with timestamp for small firms, full XML for larger ones
cards.s: Malaysia's model is binary: exempt under RM1m, or full LHDN MyInvois clearance with 55 mandatory fields in JSON/XML. There is no lightweight tier.
cards.d: Source: Thailand RD guidelines; LHDN MyInvois specification. The Thai approach lets a small trader email a PDF/A-3 with the RD's timestamp service in CC; the full system sends a stamped e-tax invoice to both parties.

cards.h: Three peers built middle paths Malaysia did not adopt
cards.text: The choice was never just 'mandate or exempt.' Three peers built middle paths Malaysia did not adopt.
Exemption is the cheapest political answer. Capacity-building is the expensive structural one. The peer models cost more — and produce more lasting compliance.

sources: CBIC Notification 10/2023 (India); IRAS GST InvoiceNow Requirement page and Committee of Supply 2026 announcement (Singapore); IMDA factsheet 2026; EY Singapore tax alert May 2025; Thailand Revenue Department guidelines; Sovos; Indonesia DGT/MoF-164; PwC Indonesia Pocket Tax Book 2025; LHDN e-invoice timeline; MDEC SME Digitalisation Grant page.
confidence: Medium-high. Each comparison fact is anchored on a primary or near-primary source. The chief risk is exchange-rate translation drift (the RM equivalents of INR, IDR, SGD, THB will change with FX moves; the source-currency figures are the durable ones). Stage 1 self-review applied — no external Stage 2-3 review run because parent issue 1984 already absorbed the external review budget for this theme.
